review_text: The gathering tear in Lecter's eye after Clarice's confession is the brilliant pinpoint of light that ultimately redeems the blackness of this dark fairy tale.
Gruesome but riveting.

One of the all-time great thrillers. Demme directs this for maximum suspense, yet the film also works as a multilayered feminist tale, with Foster's resourceful agent emerging as a beautifully conceived character.
A brilliant, unnerving work, "Lambs" proves that pop filmmaking can make both the heart and mind race.

This film is probably the best example of the crossover between the Drama/Thriller genre and the Horror genre. The scenes with Buffalo Bill are viscerally horrific, but the psychological horror of Lecter’s interrogations of Clarice are just as disturbing.
Even nicer is the spruced-up visual presentation of one of the best films of the 20th century.

Demme is not interested in blood and guts for the sake of shocking the viewer, but rather the character’s emotional and psychological reaction to the violence. [...] It is this emotional investment that makes Demme’s film so frightening.
Its impact on movies is still being felt today, and it includes arguably the best performances of both Foster and Hopkins' careers.
The crux of this excellent thriller is the battle of intellect and wills between them [Clarice Starling and Dr Hannibal Lecter].

Suspenseful and shrewd, Hopkins’ Lecter is one of the most fascinating villains in film (despite less than 20 min of screen time). In spite of his contemptible reproach and steely demeanor, there’s just something about Lecter that makes us root for him.

Most books, plays or films never give us even this much. Yet The Silence of the Lambs gives us so much that it's a shame the film doesn't find ways to deepen its concentration on the colossal aberrance of the minds of its complementary terrors.
The transformation between the two protagonists is truly outstanding. [Full review in Spanish]
The film flows from beginning to end without a single detour on its fatalist course.  [Full review in Spanish]
A failure that is a function of its success.

An exceptional thriller; a film that puts us in the shoes —and in the eyes— of its protagonist as very few other productions have managed to do, introducing us to a world of killers and cannibals and the FBI agents who chase them. Full review in Spanish.

Most viewers will be rooting for Foster's Starling all the way up to the expertly directed denouement -- a darkly whimsical ending that would have been utterly unacceptable in the old days of the Production Code.
It is a freak occurrence of the right director, the right script, and the right cast making something that transcends genre and reshapes popular culture.
Throughout the movie, Demme gets across more terror than a dozen Friday the 13ths by showing not the horrifying thing itself but the faces of people looking at horror.

Grand Guignol is too mild an expression for a work in which every scene is an exercise in repulsion. But I couldn't wait for what happened next and would have to admit that this is an excellent, if morally reprehensible, film.
The Silence of the Lambs is one of the most intoxicating and absorbing thrillers ever made.
The Silence of the Lambs is something exceedingly rare and invaluable in movies these days: an accomplished, intelligent -- and genuinely scary -- thriller.

As played by Anthony Hopkins in the performance of his film career, the amoral psychiatrist dominates the picture -- a remarkable feat, since Jodie Foster is the top-billed star and also does a superb job.

The other thing that gives Silence its special charge is Hopkins' powerfully ingratiating performance as the formidable villain. Every second he's on the screen, you can't help but feel uncomfortable.
A bone-chiller of superior intensity.
Scott Glenn's low-key portrayal keeps things anchored in normalcy and provides a scale against which the depths of Hopkins' malice and Foster's troubled nature can be measured.
As the old advertising campaign said, keep telling yourself it's only a movie. But what a movie! It is quite simply one of the very best of its kind.

Even accepting that the movie's cruelty will be its calling card, there's still the void created by no clear point-of-view character. It's nobody's movie in particular, the opposite of Michael Mann's... Manhunter.

The Silence of the Lambs is the stuff that nightmares are made of. It is, in a sense, a horror movie, but one that deserves comparison with such great works of psychological terror as The Shining, Psycho and Rosemary's Baby.
Jonathan Demme's The Silence of the Lambs is a terrific and terrifying thriller, a superbly crafted white-knuckle ride into evil psychosis.
Do not be deceived by The Silence of the Lambs' pastoral title; this is one large-toothed lion of a suspense movie, shocking, smart and savvy.

Terror, as William Blake reminds us, is the human form divine. Jonathan Demme delights in that form more than the average filmmaker, and in... The Silence of the Lambs he offers variations of that form unseen in any of his work before.
The dark tones and tense nervousness are further enhanced by Demme's oblique choreography of the action and [cinematographer Tak] Fujimoto's doom-laden compositions and camera angles.

Starling, played by a tight-lipped Foster with intelligence, fierce commitment and a half-erased rural Southern accent, and Lector, acted by Hopkins with glowing eyes and a caressing English purr, come together in a common pursuit.
If Hopkins spins The Silence of the Lambs off into the territory of ghoulish nightmares, Foster grounds it firmly in reality.
It’s a tough little B-movie, but there's more to gross you out in one of Harris’ paragraphs than in all of Demme’s two hours.
In an age of cheap chases and absurd body counts, this is one provocative exception.
A shockingly powerful thriller.

The Silence of the Lambs is one slick, sick piece of psychological manipulation. It is an ultrarealistic horror movie, expertly made, certain to start a rash of nightmares about demented killers who mutilate or devour their victims.
Does Demme's movie explore the darkness of the human psyche, or merely ride its shadowy back to the bank?

Rather than dwell on the violence, Demme relies on a well-constructed plot to keep viewers involved even when they avert their eyes... You identify more with Foster's diminutive FBI woman than with any over-muscled action hero.

The Silence of the Lambs is the Psycho of the '90s. A first-rate stomach-in-a-knot psychological thriller that will prod shaken viewers to check the back seats of their cars before they drive away from the theater.
The Silence of the Lambs is a fever dream of a movie -- beautiful and terrifying arid surreal.
Working from Thomas Harris' shocking and very scary novel, Demme goes straight to the heart of madness.
Foster and Hopkins are both very good indeed; and if the film fails to make you jump out of your skin, it may leave behind a subtler, more insidious trace.
The Silence of the Lambs is an unsatisfying film on the most basic level of all -- the story. It is sadly broken-backed.

Liberal, conservative, gross, fastidious, sly, responsible, feminist, sex-fearing, it is a particularly American compendium of impulses, a career breakthrough for Jonathan Demme that is also a Pyrrhic victory for his politics.

It has a gutsy, intelligent heroine (a difficult role admirably played by Jodie Foster), who finds police work, a socially, useful and personally therapeutic, activity, quite a change from the current run of movie cops.

There is no question that Jonathan Demme's film of Thomas Harris's best-seller is a polished production that will delight the box office. But some of the shock elements, instead of scaring you out of your skin, leave something of a nasty aftertaste.

This one is even better than you've heard. It locks on to an unpleasant subject; it offers as much relaxation as driving on black ice; yet still it is more entertaining, and more frazzling, than any film this year.

The Silence of the Lambs is a psycho-thriller for the constitutionally strong. Demme is an uncompromising film-maker. Given the raw material, he sees no reason to dilute it. Harris's readers should feel well satisfied.
